headline: Ferrowatt raises $12M Series A to retrofit small hydro dams into grid-scale batteries
summary: Ferrowatt builds control software that lets the ~2,000 small, under-used hydroelectric dams in the US behave like dispatchable grid batteries. The company is announcing a $12M Series A led by Meridian Climate Capital, alongside 18 months of performance data from 14 retrofitted dams in the Pacific Northwest.

timing.flexibility: Date is movable by up to one week
timing.peg: Announcement is timed ahead of FERC's small-hydro licensing comment period closing July 10

proofPoints.claim: 14 dams retrofitted across Oregon and Washington, dispatching power on the wholesale market for 18 months
proofPoints.evidence: Internal dispatch logs; two utility partners willing to confirm on background

proofPoints.claim: Retrofits cost 8-12% of equivalent new battery storage capacity
proofPoints.evidence: Company cost model, not yet independently verified

proofPoints.claim: $12M Series A led by Meridian Climate Capital
proofPoints.evidence: Signed term sheet; investor available for comment

proofPoints.claim: Could unlock 3.5 GW of latent storage capacity nationally

audiences: Energy policy watchers ahead of the FERC comment period

spokespeople.name: Ana Volkov
spokespeople.notes: Former dam operations engineer; strong on technical detail, untested on camera

diagnosis.summary: The story has real evidence behind it — 18 months of live dispatch data is rare for a Series A — but the funding number alone is not the story. The strongest frame is the existing-infrastructure angle with the FERC deadline as the peg. The weakest point is the lack of an on-record customer.

pitchableGrade.note: Pitchable now, but the customer proof and cost claim need tightening.

signals.label: Timeliness
signals.note: FERC small-hydro comment period closes July 10 — a real, dated peg beyond the funding announcement.

signals.label: Tension
signals.note: Implicit conflict with new-build battery storage economics; no named opponent yet.

signals.label: Evidence
signals.note: Dispatch data is strong but partners will only confirm on background. Cost claim is self-reported.

signals.label: Audience stakes
signals.note: Concrete for utilities and dam-adjacent communities; abstract for general readers.

signals.label: Novelty
signals.note: Dam retrofits-as-batteries is an under-covered angle versus the saturated battery-startup beat.

gaps: No utility customer willing to speak on the record — biggest blocker for tier-1 coverage
The 3.5 GW national estimate should not be pitched as a headline number
No photography or b-roll of an actual retrofit site yet

targetTypes.name: Energy trade press
targetTypes.whyTheyMayCare: The storage deployment gap is their running story; latent hydro capacity is a fresh answer to it.
targetTypes.proofNeeded: Dispatch data plus at least one named utility willing to confirm, even on background.

targetTypes.name: Regional business outlets
targetTypes.whyTheyMayCare: Fourteen named sites in Oregon and Washington — local land use, jobs, and tax base.
targetTypes.proofNeeded: Site access, local interviews, and photography at a retrofit.

targetTypes.name: Climate-tech newsletters
targetTypes.whyTheyMayCare: A funding round with operating revenue cuts against the announcement-economy critique.
targetTypes.proofNeeded: An independently verified cost comparison; the 8-12% claim is self-reported today.

targetTypes.name: Infrastructure reporters
targetTypes.whyTheyMayCare: FERC's small-hydro comment period gives a policy peg beyond the company news.
targetTypes.proofNeeded: A spokesperson who can speak to licensing, not just the product — no one on the roster yet.
